title: Rehabilitation in the Intensive Care Unit for Adults with Traumatic Brain Injury: A Systematic Review and Meta-analysis
one_line_summary: This meta-analysis of 13 studies (3,582 TBI patients) found ICU physical rehabilitation potentially safe (5.8% event rate) and significantly improved mobility at ICU discharge (Perme Score MD 3.25), despite confounding by TBI severity.
key_pearls: ICU physical rehabilitation for TBI patients is generally safe, with a pooled event rate of 5.8% across 3,066 sessions, primarily due to general critical illness complications.
Initiate physical rehabilitation and out-of-bed mobilization in acute TBI patients once neurosurgical, hemodynamic, and respiratory stability are achieved.
Continuously monitor ICP, MAP, CPP, heart rate, respiratory rate, SpO2, PEEP, and FiO2 during TBI rehabilitation sessions.
Immediately cease rehabilitation if patients experience an acute ICP spike, significant MAP/CPP drop, severe pain, new arrhythmias, or mechanical complications like line dislodgement.
The Perme ICU Mobility Score (0-32) significantly improved at ICU discharge (MD 3.25) with rehabilitation, making it a useful objective tracking tool.
Develop multidisciplinary, structured progressive mobility protocols tailored to daily neurological status and sedation levels for TBI patients.
population: adult patients with acute traumatic brain injury (TBI)

sections.heading: Epidemiology and Pathophysiology of TBI and ICU-Acquired Weakness
sections.content: Traumatic brain injury (TBI) is a leading global cause of death and long-term disability, with an annual incidence of 69 million, and approximately 50% of patients presenting to the hospital with TBI require ICU admission. Prolonged immobility in the ICU leads to ICU-acquired weakness (ICU-AW) in nearly 50% of critically ill patients, causing an estimated 2% loss of skeletal muscle mass per day. This symmetrical neuromuscular weakness stems from critical illness polyneuropathy, myopathy, systemic inflammation, bioenergetic failure, and impaired muscle protein synthesis. It is independently associated with prolonged mechanical ventilation, extended ICU stays, and poor long-term functional outcomes. Physical ICU rehabilitation (passive/active exercise, early mobilization) aims to preserve neuromuscular function, maintain muscle mass, and mitigate the functional consequences of ICU-AW, though it cannot fully halt the underlying catabolic and inflammatory drivers.

sections.heading: Search Strategy, Study Selection, and Baseline Characteristics
sections.content: Five databases (PubMed, Ovid Medline, AMED, CINAHL, EMBASE) were searched up to September 1, 2025. Eligible studies included adults (aged >= 16 years) admitted to the ICU with acute TBI of any severity who underwent physical rehabilitation. The included cohort comprised 13 studies (11 observational, 2 randomized controlled trials [RCTs]) representing 3,582 patients. The cohort was 69.8% male with a mean age of 48.02 +/- 7.72 years. TBI severity varied (6 studies included all severities, 4 moderate-to-severe, 3 severe only). The weighted mean baseline Glasgow Coma Scale (GCS) was 7.89 +/- 5.76 (n = 2,178). Crucially, in studies reporting baseline GCS for both groups, the rehabilitation group had a significantly lower baseline GCS than the control group (7.28 +/- 5.15 vs. 8.55 +/- 6.39, p < 0.0001), indicating that patients receiving rehabilitation were more severely injured at baseline.

sections.heading: Risk of Bias Assessment
sections.content: Observational Studies (n = 11): Evaluated using the ROBINS-E tool, 100% of these studies were judged to have a high to very-high risk of bias, primarily driven by bias in the measurement of outcomes (100%), post-exposure interventions (63.6%), and confounding (45.5%). Randomized Studies (n = 2): Evaluated using the RoB2 tool, both studies raised "some concerns" of bias due to deviations from the intended interventions.

sections.heading: Primary Outcome: Safety of Physical ICU Rehabilitation
sections.content: Safety was reported in 4 studies, with two studies reporting zero adverse events. Specific trial safety data: Riberholt 2021 (Tilt-Table Stepping) reported 24 serious adverse events (SAEs), 73 adverse events (AEs), and 4 adverse reactions across 17 of 19 intervention patients. However, there was no significant difference in total events compared to the control group (101 events in intervention vs. 101 in control). Recorded SAEs included pneumonia, delirium, sepsis, and seizures; AEs included nasogastric tube removal, pressure ulcers, urinary tract infections (UTIs), and vomiting. Yen 2022 (Progressive Mobility) reported 19 unexpected events (e.g., falls, infusion needle dislodgement) and 59 stop events (e.g., vital sign changes, dizziness) across 2,863 sessions. The overall safety event rate was calculated at 5.8% (179 events across 3,066 sessions). The authors note that many reported events (UTIs, delirium, pressure ulcers) represent general complications of critical illness rather than direct mechanical or physiological harms caused by physical therapy.
section_pearls: The overall safety event rate for physical ICU rehabilitation in TBI patients was 5.8% (179 events across 3,066 sessions).
Many reported adverse events were general critical illness complications, not direct harms from physical therapy.

sections.heading: Secondary Outcomes: Physical and Functional Metrics
sections.content: Mobility Status: Patients receiving ICU rehabilitation had significantly higher Perme ICU Mobility Scores at ICU discharge (Mean Difference [MD] 3.25, 95% CI: 2.05 to 4.44, p < 0.00001; n = 2 studies). Combined mobility status across different scales showed a non-significant trend toward improvement (Standardized Mean Difference [SMD] 0.82, 95% CI: -0.32 to 1.33, p = 0.17). Time to first mobilization was shorter but not statistically significant (MD -2.89 days, 95% CI: -14.33 to 8.54, p = 0.62). Functional Status: Functional Independence Measure (FIM) scores showed a non-significant improvement at ICU discharge (MD 4.82, 95% CI: -12.26 to 21.89, p = 0.58). Combined functional status across multiple scales was also non-significant (SMD 0.63, 95% CI: -0.72 to 1.98, p = 0.36). Muscle Function: No significant difference was observed between groups (SMD -0.02, 95% CI: -0.32 to 0.36, p = 0.91). Neurological Disability: There was a non-significant trend toward worse neurological disability at discharge in the rehabilitation group (SMD -0.24, 95% CI: -0.75 to 0.27, p = 0.35), which is highly likely to be confounded by the lower baseline GCS scores in the rehabilitation cohorts.
section_pearls: ICU rehabilitation significantly improved Perme ICU Mobility Scores at discharge (MD 3.25, p < 0.00001).
Other functional and muscle outcomes showed no significant difference, likely confounded by baseline TBI severity.

sections.heading: Secondary Outcomes: Length of Stay
sections.content: ICU Length of Stay (LOS): Trended longer in the rehabilitation group (MD 3.73 days, 95% CI: -2.51 to 9.97 days, p = 0.24). Hospital LOS: Trended longer in the rehabilitation group (MD 5.47 days, 95% CI: -5.31 to 16.24 days, p = 0.32). ICU LOS in RCTs Only: Showed no difference (MD -0.02 days, 95% CI: -12.60 to 12.60 days, p = 1.00). Confounding Factors: The apparent trend toward longer stays in the rehabilitation group is heavily confounded by "confounding by indication" (more severely injured patients with lower GCS stayed in the ICU longer, thereby having more opportunities to receive rehabilitation sessions).
section_pearls: ICU and hospital length of stay trended longer in rehabilitation groups, but this was confounded by more severe baseline TBI in these patients.
RCTs alone showed no difference in ICU LOS between rehabilitation and control groups.

sections.heading: Characteristics of Rehabilitation Interventions
sections.content: Format: Out-of-bed mobilization was the most common intervention (10 studies), ranging from passive transfers to a chair or tilt-table to active, progressive independent walking. Other modalities included ADL training, positioning, passive/active limb and trunk exercises, balance training, and family engagement. Personnel: Primarily delivered by physiotherapists (12 studies) and occupational therapists (4 studies), with some utilizing interdisciplinary teams including speech therapists, ICU nurses, and physicians. Dose & Frequency: Typically 5 to 7 days per week, 1 to 2 times per day. Session duration ranged from 15 to 60 minutes, though two studies recommended 2 to 5 hours of daily therapy. Initiation Criteria: 11 studies used clinical parameters (e.g., cessation of sedation, hemodynamic stability) to guide initiation. Timing of first session ranged from < 24 hours to 19.82 +/- 17.9 days post-admission. Safety Monitoring & Stop Criteria: 8 studies monitored vital signs during sessions. 5 studies reported explicit physiological thresholds (blood pressure, intracranial pressure [ICP], heart rate, mean arterial pressure [MAP], respiratory rate, oxygen saturation, PEEP, and FiO2). 7 studies defined explicit stop criteria (e.g., vital sign deterioration, pain, falls, unplanned extubation, or line dislodgement).
section_pearls: Rehabilitation interventions commonly involved out-of-bed mobilization, delivered by physiotherapists 5-7 days/week for 15-60 minutes.
Most studies used clinical stability criteria for initiation and explicit physiological thresholds/stop criteria for safety monitoring.

strengths_limitations: Strengths include being the first systematic review and meta-analysis on ICU rehabilitation for TBI, utilizing rigorous risk of bias tools (ROBINS-E, RoB2), and comprehensive intervention characterization via TIDieR. Limitations are significant, stemming from high bias risk in 11 observational studies, only two RCTs, extreme statistical heterogeneity (I2 = 89-100% for LOS), and prevalent confounding by indication due to lower baseline GCS in rehabilitation groups.
